Transcrição Completa: 6 8 3 million in ad spend Lessons with Jeremy Haynes
[00:00]
"All right, cool. Just real quick before we get started, um I'll do my talk here. This is the uh second half of my other talk from spending 8.3 million in ad spend from January 1st to March 31st."
[00:08]
"Uh we'll do a short and sweet bullet point style and then we'll jump into the hot seat exercise after that. Make sense?"
[00:15]
"Cool. Okay, sweet. So, to be clear, there's a few key lessons that you learn when you spend a lot of money on a month-over-month basis."
[00:23] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Lesson #1: Spending More Money is Less Risky"
NÚMEROS: 8.3 million, $50,000 test budget recomendado
VISUAL: Comparação visual entre orçamentos pequenos vs grandes, ícones de dinheiro, gráfico de probabilidade de sucesso ascendente
[00:26]
"Okay. And one of the number one recurring lessons that I consistently get reiterated to myself is that spending more money is less risky than spending less."
[00:36]
"Every time that I consistently have clients that spend, as an example, of a test budget of tens of thousands instead of like, oh, let's throw like 10K at it and see if it works."
[00:47]
"That type of client who spends more has a substantially higher probability. I won't make up a statistic and tell you a percentage of how much greater the probability is, but the probability is substantially greater in terms of odds of success of that campaign working."
[01:02]
"So, lesson one I want to make sure you understand is always defer to a higher test budget than what you likely think you need to spend. Does that make sense?"
[01:11]
"I will personally tell every client now that a good test budget is about $50,000. Okay? Every new client that we launch, we're going to have them comfortable with a number of we want you spending at least 50k on ad spend. Does that make sense?"
[01:26] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Lesson #2: The Fat Cat Closer Problem"
NÚMEROS: 3 anos consecutivos do problema, 60+ vídeos de treinamento criados
VISUAL: Ícone de vendedor preguiçoso, representação visual de "fat cat", comparação entre vendedor ideal vs realidade atual
[01:28]
"Okay. Number two, for a long time working with any kind of lead generation business where we rely on closers to get deals done, the main issue that I've had for like three years in a row now since the plague of Cole Gordon, the plague of guys like Jeremy Miner, is that salespeople do not want to be what is a real salesperson anymore."
[01:48]
"There's a plague of these salespeople. We call them fat cat closers where they don't want to go out and do any kind of outbound sale. They hate the idea of having to chase somebody."
[01:58]
"They look at chasing as derogatory. Chasing would mean following up. Chasing could also mean having to do uh as an example like somebody's interested but they're not sold yet. They view that as derogatory nowadays."
[02:11]
"For the most part, the number one thing that sales people have consistently bitched about is this person's not hot enough. I couldn't close them. They didn't show up ready to buy. They didn't watch the video. I'm not talking to people who don't watch the video."
[02:24]
"And that would make me so mad. So much so I created a 60 plus video course on sales training. Did that help me solve my problem?"
[02:32]
"Say it louder."
[02:33]
"No."
[02:34]
"Put a [fuck] in front of it. [Fuck] no it didn't."
[02:38] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Failed Solutions Timeline"
NÚMEROS: 60+ vídeos de treinamento, 3 anos tentando resolver
VISUAL: Timeline de falhas: Curso em vídeo → Coaching de vendas → Ambos falharam, X vermelho sobre cada tentativa
[02:40]
"Okay. Then I became a part-time sales trainer for a little bit where I was like, 'All right, my video course isn't doing it. I need to get on calls with these sales guys and I need to I need to be what the sales manager is lacking and I need to hold them accountable and I need to whip them back into shape and I need to make sure that they understand what the job of a salesperson is and that they actually do it. Did that help?"
[02:59]
"That's right. [Fuck] no."
[03:02] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Marketer's Real Job"
NÚMEROS: Não aplicável
VISUAL: Frase destacada em negrito, ícone de marketeiro facilitando para vendedor, fluxo simplificado de vendas
[03:04]
"Okay. Why did it not work? Because the job of a marketer is to make the salesperson's life and responsibility as easy as possible. The job of a marketer is to make the life and the job of the salesperson as easy as possible."
[03:19]
"Me doing the sales training, doing the video course for the salespeople, giving them long SOPs on how to get their show rate up."
[03:27]
"Are they consistent with any of it?"
[03:29]
"Say it more aggressive."
[03:30]
"[Fuck] no!"
[03:32]
"That's right. So, should I have continued down that path? Would continuing down that path have worked for me eventually?"
[03:39]
"No."
[03:40] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Solution: Make Them Cashiers"
NÚMEROS: Não aplicável
VISUAL: Ícone de caixa registradora, vendedor feliz sem esforço, leads "prontos para comprar" chegando
[03:42]
"So, I went the opposite direction. What's the opposite direction? Making the salesperson's job as easy as possible. Just accepting that they suck in my eyes."
[03:52]
"You know, if I just went into every deal thinking, 'Okay, these are the most trash salespeople I'll ever work with. They only want to be cashiers. They're not going to follow up. They're not going to follow up as aggressively as I want them to.'"
[04:04]
"They're not going to do value-driven follow-up. They're not going to follow up with somebody for more than a week. They're only going to focus on all their inbound leads only."
[04:13]
"If I just thought that going into every deal, what would I do differently?"
[04:18] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "New Mindset = New Strategy"
NÚMEROS: Q1 2023 (Janeiro-Março)
VISUAL: Lâmpada acesa representando insight, mudança de direção 180 graus, foco em pré-frame do lead
[04:20]
"I walked into January with that mental frame of not in a not in a disrespectful way to the sales guys. I want to make that clear in a strategic perspective to try to unlock some new thoughts and possibilities on what could make the salesperson's job how they wanted it to be."
[04:36]
"How could I make it so these guys could be cashiers? And so that's what I attempted to do all quarter."
[04:42] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Content Advertising Strategy Review"
NÚMEROS: Não aplicável
VISUAL: Diagrama de fluxo: Conteúdo → Anúncio Direct Response → Mais Conteúdo, ícones de vídeos e anúncios
[04:45]
"To be clear, most of you are familiar with my content advertising strategies. Raise your hand if you are not familiar with my content advertising strategies."
[04:52]
"So, my content advertising strategies, just to reiterate for the new folks, are either placing pieces of content before direct response ads and or after direct response ads to help with framing, warming up, objection handling, ideally before they even talk to a sales guy."
[05:11] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Forester Strategy (Upgraded)"
NÚMEROS: 72 horas de espera antes da call, 3 dias de bombardeio de conteúdo
VISUAL: Timeline visual de 3 dias, múltiplos ícones de conteúdo bombardeando o lead, calendário bloqueado
[05:13]
"And it led me to kind of tweak some of my existing strategies to be a little more aggressive. One strategy in particular I've used the most this quarter and it was called the forester."
[05:23]
"Who here, show of hands, remembers the forester ad strategy. I'll make sure I share around the SOP, but just to be clear, here's what it is as at a high-level view of this so you guys understand it."
[05:35]
"We take content, okay? And just to be clear what I mean by content. the stuff they post on their Instagram and Facebook pages that does not have call to actions that's just content."
[05:47]
"Ideally selling people on the idea of what that brand is eventually going to try to sell to them. This could be planting the right kind of views to eventually buy a product so that product makes sense in the person's life."
[05:59]
"This could be framing them on how a particular service can benefit them prior to them even being pitched on the service."
[06:06]
"So come time when they are pitched on the service or product, the right kind of thoughts are planted in their head. This makes sense."
[06:13] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Forester Flow: Content → DR Ad → MORE Content"
NÚMEROS: 72 horas, 60 peças de conteúdo
VISUAL: Fluxo em 3 etapas com setas, ícones de vídeos multiplicados, relógio mostrando 72h
[06:15]
"We show them direct response and we follow it up extremely aggressively with more content in a very short-term duration of time."
[06:23]
"How far out do you allow people to book in a call funnel right now?"
[06:27]
"I run about 72 hours usually."
[06:30]
"Okay. So I ran a test at the beginning of the year. I said, 'You know what? What if I just don't let the salespeople talk to them until three days passes by?'"
[06:39]
"That way, I had three full days to [fuck] hammer people with as many pieces of content as I possibly could to make the salesperson's job as easy as possible."
[06:48]
"Do you think it played out for me?"
[06:50]
"Yeah, it did. Worked really well."
[06:53] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Breaking Best Practices = Better Results"
NÚMEROS: 72 horas vs conectar em momentos (prática tradicional)
VISUAL: Comparação lado a lado: Método antigo (velocidade) vs Novo método (preparação), gráfico mostrando resultados superiores
[06:55]
"So, long story short, instead of allowing salespeople to talk to anybody as fast as they should, right? That's everything you've ever heard. That's everything I've ever heard. That's the best practice I've always operated under."
[07:07]
"Connect the lead to the sales team like in moments. That's the best practice, right? But does it actually work with your sales teams that you've been plugging leads into?"
[07:16]
"Right? If the SE remember if the sales people are the worst sales team you're ever going to work with in the entire world, right? They're garbage. They're all terrible nowadays. None of them are good."
[07:26]
"If we take that frame and as marketers, we say, 'Okay, why would I let a lead that's hot talk to a salesperson that's going to blow the [fuck] deal and then act like they weren't ready to buy?'"
[07:37]
"Well, they're not ready to buy because they just came in. Right? Logically, there would be some leads that would be probable at a given moment to say, 'Yeah, well, I am ready to buy right now.' Right?"
[07:48] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "The Hotline Escape Hatch"
NÚMEROS: Disponível 24/7 durante 72 horas
VISUAL: Botão vermelho de "emergência" destacado, link de escape, notificação instantânea para vendedor
[07:50]
"So, here's what I did. This is where it gets a little off the path, okay? So, make sure you pay attention to this part."
[07:56]
"I give them the option at any time via email and automated text to signal that they are ready to buy and then those people get ported to the salesperson extremely fast."
[08:07]
"Correct. So to be clear, lead comes in, okay, lead's in the business now in our CRM."
[08:13]
"The first set of emails and texts are going to say, 'Hey, at any given time, if you're ready to buy, just press this link right here, and we'll reach out to you ASAP.'"
[08:22]
"And that triggers a marketing automation set that will immediately notify the sales team, we got a hot lead, it's ready to buy, that pressed the button that says, 'I'm ready to buy.' Okay."
[08:33] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "72-Hour Content Bombardment"
NÚMEROS: 60 peças de conteúdo, 72 horas, metade vista = sucesso
VISUAL: Calendário de 3 dias com múltiplos ícones de vídeo, email, e conteúdo social bombardeando o lead
[08:35]
"When they don't press that button in between the time they came in and the 72 hours from now when their call is, I am aggressively hammering them with marketing automation via email and I'm hammering them with a fuckload of content."
[08:48]
"Now, let me define fuckload, okay? I'm talking if I have a brand that has, let's say, 60 pieces of relevant content that could help with objection handling, framing, planting the right views about the product, testimonials, anything, anything that can successfully get you to think in a better mental frame about increase."
[09:10]
"I just want to increase the probability you're ready to buy by the time you talk to the sales guy. That's the intention of every single one of those pieces of content."
[09:18]
"Even if you don't watch the content and I put 60 individual pieces of content in front of you, within 72 hours successfully, if I get half of that, I'm going to successfully establish familiarity bias, which in itself will stack the odds that I'm going to be able to have these [fuck] sales people convert you at the time that they finally talk to you."
[09:40]
"Does that make sense? You have questions on that so far?"
[09:43]
"Yeah. What channels?"

[10:56]
"So, first of all, call confirmation page will have pixel blockers by about 40 to 60% of traffic nowadays. However, we're still going to use that as a custom audience, and we're going to set it to a five-day window. Okay."
[11:10]
"We're then also going to select the direct response ads that are used to generate the booked call with a five-day window of people who watched a threshold of those ad videos of 10 seconds or more."
[11:24]
"So even though I'm technically going to get some people in there who did not book a call yet, I'm also making sure that I'm getting the people who did book a call, but I'm not expanding the audience big enough to miss the people who booked a call."
[11:37]
"And I'm also successfully increasing the odds that those people when they see the direct response ads again will book a call and come through framed because they'll get hammered by content. That make sense?"

[12:53] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Psychological Sales Manipulation (Ethical)"
NÚMEROS: Não aplicável
VISUAL: Ciclo de momentum positivo, confiança crescente = mais vendas, recency bias ilustrado
[12:55]
"So, they thought they were getting better and that they were doing better as closers. I wanted them to like build an ego."
[13:02]
"Uh I I would tell them I'd be like, 'Yeah, man. They're the same people. Like, you guys are just [fuck] crushing it now.' You know, sales people operate off recency bias. Okay?"
[13:13]
"So, I love to lie to sales people about what's happening because they they don't they don't understand. It's very important to to say this. Let me calm down a minute. Okay. Sorry. I have a lot of trauma around how much money I've lost from bad sales people. Okay."
[13:29]
"So, long story short, okay, the salespeople when I say I I like to lie to sales people, what I'm trying to say is like I want to play into them feeling more confident, more certain. I want to play it."
[13:41]
"They all operate off recency bias which is which is like if I am doing good they continue to do good. If they start doing bad they continue to do bad. Okay. It's the game of momentum."
[13:52]
"So when I can have them under the mental frame of like I am doing great because of me as a closer my skills that's incredible for increasing and and making it a feeling of perpetuity from there forward that they're great."
[14:05]
"You know, I'm trying to constantly like create that inertia for them and I'm not trying to make them think that it's by my fault or doing."
[14:12]
"But I always tell the business owner that way they don't [fuck] it up because I'm always telling the business owner what I'm doing and what I'm spending money on obviously."
[14:20]
"So I tell them I'm like do not tell those sales people what's going on. [16:33]

[16:46]

[16:55]

[17:04]

[17:43]

VISUAL: Comparação: Todos vídeos em 1 ad set vs 1 vídeo por ad set, prós e contras de cada
[18:16]

[18:24]
"However, I also have recently tested putting one video inside of one adset and do an [ABO] instead. That just ensures the reach of that specific video, but it also inflated the cost a little bit for the cost per engagement or cost per view."
[18:39]
"Although, it's pretty negligible because it goes from like a tenth of a penny to maybe like a penny or two."
[18:45]
"Are you excluding per ad set from each video if they want?"
[18:49]
"No. See, that's what I didn't like about doing it on the per adset level is it was like far more just more work, you know, to set it up that way."
[18:58]
"And it was a pain in the ass because I'd have some people that have like [fuck] 400 pieces of content I'm doing what's with."
[19:04]

[19:11]

[19:38] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Budget Allocation Breakdown ($50k/mês)"
NÚMEROS: $300-500 conteúdo inicial, $700-1,100/dia DR, $300-800/dia retargeting conteúdo, $0.02-0.04 custo
VISUAL: Gráfico de pizza mostrando distribuição de budget, percentuais claros para cada fase
[19:40]
"So so the initial content bucket that's where so out of that $50,000 let's call it about about give or take give or take like $300 to maybe $500 of that's going to go towards the first step of targeting the people that we want to bring into the direct response campaign with content."
[20:00]
"And then it's important to note like the direct response give or take the objective will dictate what the budget's going to be. But just to give you a range like if it's like a purchase objective once again like purchase objectives can vary wildly depending on what your cost per purchase is."
[20:16]
"But that's where a good bulk of the budget will go. We'll typically put like seven to sometimes like $1,100 a day towards the direct response portion of that."
[20:26]
"And it's very important to understand like we're specifically targeting people who have at least seen an initial piece of content."
[20:33]
"Then we're typically split testing a cold audience who has not seen the content against it as well. So we ensure that that budget actually is spent and not inflated just by targeting the people who have seen content."
[20:45]
"And then the remaining budget which can typically be like another $300 to sometimes upwards of $800 a day is just going towards hammering the people who have seen the direct response or who have actually booked a call and targeting them with all that content."
[21:00]
"And we do a higher budget on that and it typically averages like two to four pennies rather than like a tenth of a penny or a penny because we're inflating the cost on that a bit."
[21:11]
"If we see an inflated cost like per engagement per view, excuse me. If we see an inflated cost on that, we'll lower the budget a little bit just because we we know what a cost per engagement or cost per video view should be. So if that statistics inflated, we pull it down a tad."
[21:26]
"Does that make sense?"
[21:27]
"Yeah."
[21:28]
"Okay. Have you seen a specific type of content being referenced more frequently on a sales call or"
[21:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Long-Form Content Performance"
NÚMEROS: 1h55min duração, $1.14 para visualização completa, Fresh and Fit podcast
VISUAL: Ícone de vídeo longo, comparação short-form vs long-form, custo impressionantemente baixo destacado
[21:37]
"Oh yeah, great question. So it won't just be short form content we'll use. Ideally, we have a lot of long form content that we can specifically deploy on Tik Tok and on Facebook."
[21:47]
"So Instagram, we can't have as long of content obviously, but Facebook, like as an example, I have a video that's me on the Fresh and Fit podcast. Hour and 55 minutes, costs a $1.14 right now to get somebody to watch the full hour and 55 minutes."
[22:03]
"I'll pay a $1.50 every day of the week to get somebody to watch that full video and then talk to my sales team. Does that make sense?"
[22:11]
"But I also there's more short form content than long form content. So any long form content that we have uh we'll make sure that we promote and we also obviously have just an abundance of short form stuff. So yeah, anything that fits a category in that example. Yeah."
[22:26]
"Got it."
[22:27]
"I didn't hear you mention YouTube."

NÚMEROS: Não aplicável
VISUAL: Comparação de mentalidade do usuário: YouTube (focado, pulando ads) vs Facebook/IG/TikTok (scroll passivo, aberto a sugestão)

[22:42]

[24:16]
"It's I will say does it make it rebuild?"

[24:32]

[28:05]

[28:22]
"About 60 to maybe even 70% of that's going to go towards the direct response and then the other like 20 to 30% of it's going to go towards the retargeting content for that."

[28:38] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Philosophy Summary: Make Salespeople Cashiers"
NÚMEROS: Não aplicável
VISUAL: Livro "For Dummies", vendedor como caixa registradora, aceitar a realidade vs lutar contra ela
[28:40]
"Okay. If we were once again, if we were writing a section for how to make a salesperson's job as easy as possible for dummies, we don't want to act like they need to do more sales training."
[28:51]
"We don't want to act like they should be better than they currently are. We'd rather act like they are how they are."
[28:58]
"And like I said, defer to the fact that they're likely the worst you've ever worked with. So, you unlock even better ideas about how to make their job easier to make it so they can be the cashier they want to be. Does that make sense? Do you get what I'm saying?"
[29:13]
"Okay. So, moral of the story, this changed the game for me in this first quarter. Once again, I've lost a tremendous amount of money from trying to do the opposite, which is look, you're getting qualified leads."
[29:25]
"You're getting people to fill out this [fuck] application that you have the questions defined for, and you're not closing people that come through qualified."
[29:33]
"And in your own words, you just constantly say that they're not ready to buy. So, they're pretty much telling you what they want. They only want to do they only want to talk to people who are ready to buy."
[29:44]
"So, if that's the case, and you and I both know that's not probable to happen on a [fuck] sales call, they lose the ability to talk to a majority of people until 72 hours passes and we aggressively hammer those [fuck] with as much framing content as we possibly can to increase the odds that they do show up ready to buy."
[30:04]
"Does that make sense? Real quick, so you can reflect on this with me. What is something that you've bought that when you showed up, you were ready to buy?"
[30:14]
"That's great. What made you do that?"
[30:17]
"I didn't talk to you, you know."

[31:13] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Rolls-Royce Case Study: Brand Framing"
NÚMEROS: $98k, $1,000 guarda-chuva
VISUAL: Logo Rolls-Royce, posicionamento em filmes/mídia, percepção de luxo construída ao longo do tempo
[31:15]
"Oh, yeah. It's like, you know, when I when I bought the rolls, I had no resistance at all. You know, I called them and I said, 'This car, I'm ready to go. Tell me what I need to wire. Let's make it happen.' You know, that salesperson didn't have to do anything. Okay?"
[31:30]
"I didn't I even sent them a tow truck because I didn't want to wait for them to [fuck] take however long they were going to take. I did every single thing myself in that process. Made it as easy as I could."
[31:40]
"And when I think about it, you know, there's a lot of framing that goes into how we all interpret Rolls-Royce, right? It's like, what the [fuck] makes that umbrella $1,000?"
[31:50]
"You know, it's the brand, obviously. But how do we build that kind of perception about something? You know, they've strategically placed their product in all kinds of different things."
[32:00]
"The guys and girls that you've looked up to in your life, the movies, the little pieces of content, where those products are and where you see them, when you see them in your life, you know, when you finally get the opportunity to see one and touch it, you know, like what a magical experience that was."
[32:16]
"It's like these kinds of things. At the time I finally get to the point where I'm ready to purchase it, it's like I'm ready to go, man. Take my 98k and give me the [fuck] car. Does that make sense?"
[32:26]
"Groceries. It's a little different because you need food. I don't need a Rolls-Royce."
[32:31]
"Example, I bought AirPod Max."
[32:34]

[32:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "AirPod Max Example: Content-Driven Purchase"
NÚMEROS: Não aplicável
VISUAL: YouTube como plataforma de research, vídeos de review influenciando decisão, interesse → conteúdo → compra
[32:37]
"And I wasn't sure if I wanted to buy it. So, it wasn't like a deal, but content that I looked up on YouTube and [fuck] like that was what made walk in just"
[32:47]
"So you had a level of interest that was taken to I'm ready to buy by YouTube videos. Now imagine that brand actually knew that and they advertised to people to buy their headphones, hammered them with [fuck] content when they left the site and didn't buy and then gave them an opportunity to come back to the site and buy."
[33:06]
"What would the probability be of a purchase? A lot higher, right?"
[33:10]
"So point is that specific lesson was so profound because I did the opposite of what I had been doing for the past two years."
[33:18]
"You know, I was on track to probably lose years of my life from how stressed sales people were making me and all the things I was trying to do about it."
[33:26]
"But instead, once again, I just played into the opposite and that ended up benefiting me more. Does that make sense?"
[33:33]
"Okay, cool."
[33:35] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Lesson #3: Treat Them Like a Customer Post-Purchase"
NÚMEROS: Não aplicável
VISUAL: Jornada pós-compra, experiência de unboxing, onboarding, valor imediato estabelecido
[33:37]
"Okay, third main thing that I pulled as a lesson from all this. Once again, lesson one, spending more money is always less risky than spending less money and trying to be safe."
[33:47]
"Lesson two, once again, is all the framing lessons and not allowing the terrible salespeople to talk to the leads and ruin them. Frame them first."
[33:56]
"Third lesson, value after the purchase has to immediately be established to substantially increase the probability of repeat purchases or upsell potential."
[34:07]
"So, to be clear, I've always called this a treat them like a customer campaign."
[34:12]
"How many of you have bought something and then immediately after you buy it, you're like, 'Where the [fuck] are the instructions on how to use this thing?'"
[34:20]
"Or like, you buy something and then you're like, 'Does this company hate me? Like, why aren't they talking to me? I just gave them money.'"
[34:27]
"Have you ever had that happen where there's like a fall off after a purchase? There's like a big disconnect."
[34:33] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Apple Unboxing Experience Case Study"
NÚMEROS: Não aplicável
VISUAL: Caixa Apple com fita suave, experiência de unboxing premium, emails de especialista em produto, caixas guardadas
[34:35]
"Whereas some of the most the best companies, think about when you bought that Apple computer, you buy it, you get the tracking emails, it finally [fuck] shows up. Think about that Apple unboxing experience."
[34:47]
"You know, the little piece of smooth tape that comes off the box, you [fuck] pull the box off. It's like vacuum sealed and then it drops and you're like, 'Fuck yeah.' You know, and you pull it out."
[34:57]
"How many of you keep Apple boxes in your house?"
[35:00]
"What does Apple also do after you buy the product that you've probably never done with them because we're all young here? You probably never used them.'"
[35:17]
"You know, like I said, we don't need that. We know how to use our phones and computers. But how many old people you think take them up on there?"
[35:24]

[35:37]
"You think it increases the probability, leaves it the same, or decreases that probability of a repeat purchase or another purchase? Yeah, obviously increases it."
[35:46] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Reality Check: Most Companies Fail Post-Purchase"
NÚMEROS: Não aplicável
VISUAL: Email genérico de acesso, desaparecimento da marca, falta de onboarding, oportunidade perdida
[35:48]
"Most people that I work with when I come in and I get all that other [fuck] I just talked about done first and I look at okay what happens after they buy."
[35:57]
"There's like a marketing automation email. It's like here's access or like all right here it is vanish gone. The person just gets like the general emails from the brand from that point forward."
[36:07]
"Doesn't make any sense, right? They should be treated like a customer. They should be taught how to get the most value out of whatever it is that they just purchased."
[36:16]
"What does that do when it comes to that person spending more money with us? Increases the odds that when we finally hit them with the, 'Hey, we got this that could help you more or hey, we got this other thing,' they're definitely going to consider it at a much higher rate. Does that make sense to you?"
[36:32] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Food Vendor Case Study: $40k/Year Customer"
NÚMEROS: $10k por trimestre, $40k anual, entrega em 20 minutos, hotline dedicada
VISUAL: Serviço excepcional → upsells naturais → cliente vitalício, relacionamento vs transação
[36:34]
"The people that we buy the food from every time, they do a great job, right? They [fuck] come over here and deliver it themselves. They're always They tell us, 'Hey man, you run out of food or something, call us. We'll be there within 20 minutes with more food for you.' They're [fuck] sweet."
[36:51]
"You know what they offered me the other day? They were like, 'Dude, by the way, we can come out there and we can [fuck] cook some stuff live for people in addition to all the catering we do.'"
[37:00]
"And I was like, 'Yeah, it sounds pretty sweet, man.' I was at a much higher probability to consider that in comparison to if they just delivered food laxidasically, you know, didn't give me the the phone a food hotline, you know what I mean?"
[37:13]
"Substantially higher probability that I'm willing to spend more money with them like we do every quarter. You know, we spend 10 grand on food. Just so you all know, every single quarter with them. I'm a $40,000 a year customer to that business because they treat me like a customer after I buy and they try, it's key word here, they try to ensure that I have the best experience possible after I've given them money."
[37:37]
"Does that make sense? Most people do not do this. If you go back and you look at any company you're working with, you look at your own companies, I bet for mo the most part the onboarding experience where that experience right after they purchased is non-existent or [fuck]"
[37:54]
"Does that make sense?"

[40:28] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "RESULTADO FINAL Q1 2023"

[40:46]

[41:02]

[41:08] 📊 SLIDE DETECTADO ───────────────────────────── TEXTO: "Backend Automation = Expanded Revenue Share"
NÚMEROS: Revshare expandido: front-end + backend + LTV total
VISUAL: Modelo de compensação expandido, incentivo alinhado, ganha-ganha cliente-agência
[41:10]
"Yeah, exactly. Because to be clear, what I'll do with a client typically on the front end is where we get paid our rev share is I'll tell them I'll be like, 'Look, we see a huge lift in conversions, lifetime value increasing, cross sales when we come in and we handle some of the backend stuff.'"
[41:26]
"If they say they're open to that, we'll spend that effort and time executing for them like some back-end automation to increase probabilities of upsell."
[41:34]
"But the incentive for me to do that is I expand my rev share from only being paid on front-end revenue to being paid on like the entirety of what that customer becomes worth."
[41:43]
"Other questions?"
[41:45]
"All right, cool. Yeah, give a round of applause and we'll go hang out."
📊 RESUMO EXECUTIVO
NÚMEROS PRINCIPAIS:
$8.3 milhões investidos em ad spend (Janeiro 1 - Março 31)
$46 milhões+ em retorno gerado
ROAS mínimo: 5:1 (típico com a estratégia)
Orçamento de teste recomendado: $50,000
Janela de espera: 72 horas antes da call
Conteúdo bombardeado: 60+ peças em 3 dias
Frequência ideal: 20+ impressões em 3 dias
Custo por visualização completa (1h55min): $1.14
Taxa de fechamento: 30-50% (bons times), 14% (piores)
Produtos vendidos: $5k-$50k
Bloqueio de pixel: 40-60% do tráfego
Cliente vitalício exemplo: $40k/ano (food vendor)
ESTRATÉGIAS E SISTEMAS:
The Forester Strategy (Upgraded)
Conteúdo inicial → Direct Response → Bombardeio de conteúdo por 72h
Calendário bloqueado: não permite booking antes de 3 dias
"Hotline" de escape: botão "pronto para comprar" disponível 24/7
Audiências Customizadas
Janela de 5 dias
10+ segundos de visualização em DR ads
Página de confirmação + engajamento em vídeos
Estrutura de Campanhas (até 6 total)
2 campanhas iniciais: Video Views + Engagement (conteúdo frio)
1 campanha Direct Response
2 campanhas retargeting: Video Views + Engagement (pós-DR)
Distribuição de Budget ($50k/mês exemplo)
20% conteúdo inicial ($300-500)
60-70% Direct Response ($700-1,100/dia)
20-30% retargeting conteúdo ($300-800/dia)
Treat Them Like a Customer Campaign
Onboarding imediato pós-compra
Ensinar uso do produto/serviço
Comunicação contínua
Aumenta LTV, upsell e cross-sell
ESTRUTURA:
Plataformas usadas: Facebook, Instagram, TikTok, Email
Plataforma NÃO usada: YouTube (usuários treinados para pular ads)
Tipos de conteúdo:
Short-form (maioria)
Long-form (podcasts 1h+)
Testimonials
Case studies
Outcome videos
Behind-the-scenes
Process demonstrations
Objetivos de campanha: Engagement + Video Views
CBO vs ABO: CBO preferido (todos vídeos em 1 ad set)
Automação: Email sequences + SMS + CRM tasks
DADOS FINANCEIROS:
Investimento total Q1: $8.3M
Retorno total Q1: $46M+
ROAS médio: ~5.5:1
Custo por engajamento: $0.02-0.04
Custo por view (retargeting): $0.01-0.02
Custo por view completa (long-form): $1.14
Ticket médio produtos: $5k-$50k
Modelo de compensação: RevShare (front + backend)
FILOSOFIA CENTRAL:
Gastar mais é menos arriscado que gastar menos
Aceitar que vendedores são "caixas" e facilitar o trabalho deles
Frame o lead ANTES de falar com vendedor
Valor pós-compra = repeat purchases + upsells
DURAÇÃO: ~41 minutos
FORMATO: Apresentação ao vivo com Q&A interativo
